text: In salinized solutions all plants take up Na+ from the solution surrounding the roots. Once Na+ enters the symplast, it moves to several locations within the plant cells, tissues and organs. Sodium can act as an osmotically effective ion that decreases the water potential of the plant cell, as well as being toxic. Thus Na+ uptake and translocation has to be tightly regulated in salt-resistant plants. The present assessment of the salt-resistance/salt-sensitivity of plants to salt stress indicates that it is not associated with transport mechanisms that differ in any way from those present in salt-sensitive species, but with regulation of sodium influx and translocation within the plant. Increasing numbers of salt stress-regulatory mechanisms are being revealed including several possible signals such as calcium ions, cyclic nucleotides, ROS and plant hormones, which control different levels of gene expression as well as post-transcriptional regulation and modification of salt-stress-associated proteins. The purpose of this review is to summarize present knowledge of Na+ transport within plants under salt-stress conditions and the regulation of these processes, particularly in the crucial salt-stress acquisition phase of salt-stressed plants.
